notes.anchor: son of the Prince of the Powers of the Air
notes.note: The title echoes Ephesians 2:2, where Satan is called the prince of the power of the air. Melville hangs it on Tashtego, the Gay Head harpooneer, imagining that a Puritan eye might have read so wild and free a figure as something devilish, the period's reflex of casting the non-Christian outsider as demonic.

Ahasuerus is the Persian king of the Book of Esther, often identified with Xerxes, famous in scripture for a vast empire and overwhelming royal majesty. Melville gives the name to Daggoo, the towering African harpooneer, casting him as an imperial figure of grandeur rather than a servant.

Tic-Dolly-row is Stubb's comic mangling of tic douloureux, the French name for trigeminal neuralgia, a sudden stabbing pain along the nerves of the face (Webster). He reaches for the word to describe a nagging conscience as something just as involuntary, sourceless, and tormenting.

notes.anchor: thrones of the sea-loving Danish kings
notes.note: The narwhal's long spiral tusk was once sold as the horn of the unicorn and prized above its weight in gold; Danish royalty had a coronation throne built of it, still kept at Rosenborg Castle. Scoresby's account of the Arctic fishery records the narwhal and the high value set on its ivory in the northern trade.

notes.anchor: Ahab seemed a pyramid, and I, like a blazing fool, kept kicking at it
notes.note: Queen Mab is the fairy of dreams in Mercutio's speech in Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet (1.4), who rides through sleepers' brains and makes them dream of their own wishes and fears. Melville names Stubb's strange dream-chapter after her, marking the kicked pyramid and the merman as a comic vision in that tradition.

William Scoresby (1789-1857) was a British Arctic whaling captain and naturalist whose Account of the Arctic Regions (1820) gave the first full scientific treatment of the Greenland right whale and its fishery. Ishmael leans on him as the great authority on the northern whale, while noting he knew little of the sperm whale.

Thomas Beale was a ship's surgeon on Pacific sperm-whalers whose Natural History of the Sperm Whale (1839) was the fullest first-hand scientific account of the animal in Melville's day. Melville drew on Beale throughout the book and names him here as one of the few writers who put the living sperm whale credibly before a reader.

notes.anchor: On that Ahaz-dial the shadow often goes back
notes.note: The dial of Ahaz is the sundial in 2 Kings 20:11 and Isaiah 38:8 on which God makes the shadow move ten degrees backward as a sign to the dying King Hezekiah. Ishmael lays that miracle-language over the Killer or Finback whale's shadow-casting fin, dressing a plain natural fact in Old Testament wonder.

Specksnyder is Dutch for fat-cutter, the title of the chief harpooneer in the old Dutch Greenland whale fishery, who once shared command of the ship with the navigating captain. The word lived on in British Arctic whaling as Specksioneer, though by Melville's time the post had shrunk to senior harpooneer.

notes.anchor: To have been Belshazzar, King of Babylon
notes.note: Belshazzar is the Babylonian king of Daniel 5 who feasts from the sacred vessels looted from the Temple, until a disembodied hand writes his doom on the wall. Melville raises him as the very image of power wielded at a table, then says Ahab's silent mastery over his officers at dinner outdoes even that royal grandeur.
